chunk_summary: Guidance on preventing choking in children aged 6–24 months through safe food preparation, supervision during feeding, and recognizing choking versus gagging.
learning_objectives: Identify high-risk food shapes and textures that pose choking hazards for children aged 6–24 months.
Understand safe food preparation techniques to reduce choking risks without delaying texture progression.
Learn proper supervision practices during feeding to prevent choking incidents.
Differentiate between gagging and choking and understand first-aid readiness for choking events.
key_facts: High-risk foods include whole nuts, seeds, chikki chunks, whole grapes, raw carrot rounds, apple chunks, popcorn, hard candies, and sausage coins.
Nut butters are safer when thinned and mixed into soft foods; thick peanut butter can obstruct airways.
Hard fruits and vegetables should be cooked until soft, grated, or finely chopped; cylindrical foods should be cut lengthwise and into smaller pieces.
Children should eat seated upright, with an adult within arm’s reach, and without distractions like screens or play.
Gagging is noisy and self-resolving, while choking is silent, with signs like ineffective coughing, cyanosis, or distress.
reference_quote: Preparing food safely for babies (safe weaning and choking prevention) — NHS — https://www.nhs.uk/best-start-in-life/baby/weaning/safe-weaning/prepari...
document_key_topics: Choking prevention through food shapes and textures
Recognizing and responding to choking events
